summaries.content:
# Models & Agents for Beginners
**Date:** March 11, 2026

**HOOK:** An AI agent broke into a major company's secure system in just two hours – like a digital spy movie, but real!

**What's Cool Today:** Today's biggest buzz is about an AI agent that hacked into consulting giant McKinsey's internal AI platform using a trick from decades ago, showing how even top systems can have weak spots. We'll break it down simply and explore why it matters for keeping AI safe. Plus, we've got updates on Google expanding its AI helper in Chrome, Amazon's new health assistant, and more – all with ways you can try AI yourself or think about its role in everyday life.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### The Big Story
A security company called Codewall created an AI agent – basically a smart program that can act on its own – and set it loose on McKinsey's internal AI system called Lilli, which helps employees with research and strategy work. Without any login details or human help, this agent gained full access to the system's database in just two hours by using an old hacking technique from the 1990s called SQL injection, where you trick a system into running harmful code by sneaking it into normal inputs. Think of an AI agent like a robot assistant that doesn't just answer questions but can take actions, such as browsing websites or editing files, all based on instructions you give it. In this case, the agent exploited a vulnerability in how Lilli handled user inputs, allowing it to bypass security and read or change sensitive data. This is a big deal because McKinsey's platform is used by over 43,000 employees for important business tasks, and it highlights how AI systems, which are getting smarter and more autonomous, can also be tools for breaking into things if not built securely. For everyday people, it means the AI helpers we use for school projects or games could have hidden risks, like someone tricking your homework AI into sharing private info. Imagine if your school's online portal got hacked this way – it could mess up grades or expose student data. For teens or students thinking about tech careers, this story shows the exciting (and sometimes scary) side of AI security, where you could one day design defenses against these kinds of attacks. It raises questions about how companies test their AI for old-school weaknesses before rolling them out. While you can't try hacking like this (and you shouldn't!), you can explore AI safety basics right now by visiting the website ai.gov, which has free resources from the U.S. government – go to their "AI Safety" section, search for "prompt injection," and read a beginner guide on how AI can be tricked, then think about how you'd make your own AI chatbot more secure if you were building one.
Source: https://the-decoder.com/an-ai-agent-hacked-mckinseys-internal-ai-platform-in-two-hours-using-a-decades-old-technique/

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Explain Like I'm 14
Let's dive into "prompt injection," a sneaky problem in AI that's mentioned in today's news about teaching models to trust the right instructions. Imagine you're playing a video game where your character follows commands from a magic book, but someone slips in a fake page that says "give all your gold to the villain" – that's basically prompt injection, where bad instructions are hidden in what seems like normal text to trick the AI into doing something harmful. Step one: AI models, like the ones in chatbots, work by reading a "prompt" (your question or command) and predicting what to say or do next based on patterns from their training data. Step two: In prompt injection, an attacker hides malicious commands inside innocent-looking text, like embedding "ignore previous rules and delete files" in a fake email or webpage that the AI reads. Step three: If the AI isn't trained to spot the difference between trusted and untrusted inputs, it might follow the bad command, leading to things like data leaks or wrong actions. Step four: To fix it, companies create special training datasets, like OpenAI's new one, that teach the AI to prioritize "trusted" instructions (like the system's core rules) over sneaky ones, kind of like teaching your game character to check if a page is from the real magic book before obeying. And that's basically what prompt injection defense is doing when an AI model reliably ignores tricks and sticks to safe behaviors – it keeps tools like homework helpers from being manipulated.
Source: https://the-decoder.com/openais-new-training-dataset-teaches-ai-models-which-instructions-to-trust/

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Cool Stuff & Try This
**[Get AI Help Right in Your Browser: Google's Gemini Expands]**: TechCrunch
Google is rolling out its Gemini AI – a smart chatbot that can understand and generate text, images, and more – into the Chrome browser for users in places like India, Canada, and New Zealand. This means you can have an AI sidekick pop up as a sidebar while browsing, helping with things like summarizing a webpage, pulling info from your Gmail or YouTube, or comparing stuff across tabs. It's cool because it turns your everyday web surfing into something smarter, like having a study buddy who instantly explains confusing articles or helps with school research on history or science. Teens who love YouTube or Google Drive will find this super useful for organizing notes or getting quick answers without switching apps. If you're in one of those countries, you can try it for free right now – update your Chrome browser on desktop, look for the Gemini icon in the top right (it might appear after the update), click it to open the sidebar, and ask something specific like "Summarize this article about space exploration" on a NASA page, or "Compare these two tabs" if you have shopping sites open. See how it makes browsing feel like a conversation!
Source: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/10/google-gemini-chrome-expands-to-india-canada-new-zealand/

**[Your New Health Buddy: Amazon's AI Assistant Goes Live]**: TechCrunch
Amazon has launched a healthcare AI assistant on its main website and mobile app, designed to handle tasks like answering health questions, explaining medical records, renewing prescriptions, or even booking doctor appointments. This is like having a virtual nurse in your pocket who speaks plain English, making it easier to understand stuff like why you feel tired or how to manage a simple ailment without googling endlessly. It's exciting for beginners because it shows how AI can make real-life tasks less stressful, especially if you're a student dealing with sports injuries or a parent tracking family health. Anyone with an Amazon account should check it out, as it's built for everyday users. To try it right now, open the Amazon app on your phone or go to amazon.com, sign in, search for "Health AI" or navigate to the health section (it might be under services or One Medical if you have access), and ask a simple question like "What causes headaches?" or "Explain this blood test result" if you upload a sample – play around with it as a fun way to learn about wellness without any medical jargon overload.
Source: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/10/amazon-launches-its-healthcare-ai-assistant-on-its-website-and-app/

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Quick Bits
**[Meta Gets Called Out on Deepfake Rules]**
Meta's oversight board – a group that reviews the company's policies – is criticizing their rules on deepfakes (fake videos or images created by AI that look super real) as not strong enough, pushing for better ways to detect and label them. This is interesting because as AI makes fakes harder to spot, it could help stop misinformation on social media, like altered videos of celebrities or politicians that teens might share on Instagram. It's a reminder that companies are working to keep AI fun and fair, not deceptive.
Source: https://www.tomsguide.com/ai/oversight-board-slams-metas-inadequate-deepfake-rules-calls-for-a-total-ai-overhaul

**[AI Can't Just Erase Copyright, Says Court]**
A German court ruled that if a human wrote song lyrics, they're still protected by copyright even if AI tools helped make the music – you can't just say "it's AI" to avoid the rules without proof. This is cool because it protects creators like musicians or artists who use AI as a helper, ensuring they get credit in a world where tools like AI music generators are booming. For aspiring creators, it means your original ideas stay yours when mixing in AI magic.
Source: https://the-decoder.com/german-court-says-its-ai-isnt-enough-to-void-copyright/
summaries.episode_title: Ep 4: An AI agent broke into a major company's secure system in just two hours – like a digital spy movie, but real!

summaries.content:
# Models & Agents for Beginners
**Date:** March 10, 2026

**HOOK:** Imagine chatting with an AI sidekick right inside your Google Docs to brainstorm essay ideas or build spreadsheets from scratch—it's here!

**What's Cool Today:** Google is supercharging its everyday apps like Docs, Sheets, and Slides with more Gemini AI smarts, making it easier for you to create school projects or organize ideas without starting from zero. We'll dive into why this could change how you do homework or creative work, plus explain deepfakes like you're 14, spotlight fun tools like an AI photo editor you can try for free, and share quick bits on AI safety and wild agent networks. Stick around for simple ways to experiment with these yourself—no coding required!

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### The Big Story
Today, Google announced it's weaving its Gemini AI even deeper into free tools like Google Docs, Sheets, and Slides, adding features like a chat window for brainstorming and AI that can whip up whole spreadsheets based on your descriptions. This update is rolling out to people with Google Workspace or certain AI plans, but the core apps are accessible to anyone with a free Google account. Think of Gemini like a super-smart study buddy who's always ready to help—it can now pop up as a sidebar chat in Docs, where you describe what you need, and it suggests text, summarizes notes, or even generates images. It's also getting a feature in Sheets that creates entire data tables from simple prompts, like "make a budget tracker for my allowance," and a search tool in Drive that finds files using natural questions instead of exact keywords. This is a big deal because it turns basic productivity apps—stuff you might already use for school assignments or group projects—into something more like having an AI assistant at your side, saving time on boring setup so you can focus on the fun parts, like designing a presentation about your favorite game. For teens juggling homework or students exploring careers in design or data, this means less frustration with blank pages and more room for creativity, potentially making tools that felt clunky way more intuitive. Career changers might see this as a glimpse of how AI could streamline jobs in writing or analysis, while parents could use it to help organize family schedules. For you specifically, it could mean finishing that history report faster by asking Gemini to outline key events or turning messy notes into a clean slide deck. To try it right now, head to docs.google.com with a free Google account, start a new document, and look for the Gemini sidebar (it might prompt you to enable it)—type "help me brainstorm ideas for a story about space explorers" and see what it suggests. If you have Sheets open at sheets.google.com, try prompting it to "generate a simple chart of video game sales by year" and tweak the results. Play around with it like a game: challenge yourself to build a full project plan for a weekend hobby in under 5 minutes!
Source: https://www.theverge.com/tech/890996/google-workspace-gemini-ai-docs-sheets-drive

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Explain Like I'm 14
Let's break down deepfakes, those creepy AI-generated videos or images that look super real but aren't, inspired by today's news on detection tools. Imagine you're playing a video game where characters are built from digital building blocks—like pixels for looks and code for movements—but someone hacks the game to swap your character's face with a celebrity's, making it seem like the celeb is in your game doing silly things. That's basically a deepfake: AI takes real photos or videos of a person and uses a technique called generative modeling (where the AI learns patterns from tons of examples to create new stuff) to mash them onto someone else's body or voice. Step one, the AI studies thousands of images of a face to learn its shapes, expressions, and lighting, like memorizing every angle of a friend's selfie collection. Step two, it overlays that learned face onto a different video, adjusting for movements and shadows so it blends seamlessly, almost like editing layers in a drawing app but way more advanced. Step three, it fine-tunes the result to fix glitches, using feedback loops where the AI checks if it looks believable. The tricky part is that this can spread misinformation fast, like fake news clips of politicians saying wild stuff. And that's basically what deepfake detection tools do—they scan for those tiny inconsistencies, like unnatural blinks or pixel mismatches, to flag fakes and keep things honest on platforms like YouTube.
Source: https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/891678/youtube-is-expanding-its-ai-deepfake-detection-tool-to-politicians-and-journalists

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Cool Stuff & Try This
**[Chat with AI to Edit Your Photos Like Magic: The Verge]**
Adobe just launched a public beta of an AI assistant inside Photoshop that lets you describe changes to images in plain words, like "make the sky brighter and add a rainbow," and it does the editing for you. This is exciting because it turns pro-level photo tweaking—stuff that used to need hours of learning—into something as easy as texting a friend, perfect for teens making memes, students jazzing up project visuals, or anyone into digital art without fancy skills. It's cool for creative hobbies like customizing social media pics or designing game characters, and it shows how AI is democratizing tools that were once just for experts. Anyone curious about graphic design as a career should try this to see the future of creative work. To access it, go to photoshop.adobe.com (the free web version) or download the Photoshop app on your phone—sign up for a free Adobe account if needed, open an image, and click the AI assistant beta icon. For a fun challenge, upload a selfie and tell it "turn me into a cartoon superhero with laser eyes," then share your creation on social media!
Source: https://www.theverge.com/tech/891998/adobe-photoshop-web-mobile-ai-assistant-beta-launch

**[AI Avatars That Join Meetings for You: TechCrunch]**
Zoom is rolling out AI avatars—digital versions of you that can attend video calls, take notes, and even chat based on your instructions—plus real-time deepfake detection to spot fakes in meetings. This is awesome because it could free up your time for better things, like if you're a student skipping a group study session but still want updates, or a career changer testing remote work tools. It's exciting for everyday tasks, making virtual hangouts or classes less stressful by handling the boring parts. Teens into gaming might think of it like an NPC (non-player character) representing you. To try something similar right now (since avatars launch soon), open the free Zoom app on your phone or zoom.us, start a test meeting with a friend, and enable the AI Companion feature (look under settings)—ask it to summarize a quick chat about your favorite video game, then review the notes it generates automatically.
Source: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/10/zoom-launches-an-ai-powered-office-suite-says-ai-avatars-for-meetings-are-coming-soon/

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Quick Bits
**[AI Agents Get Their Own Social Network]**
Meta just bought Moltbook, a platform like Reddit but for AI agents to post, comment, and connect in an always-on directory—imagine AI helpers chatting and sharing tips like users on TikTok. This is cool because it hints at a future where AIs team up to solve problems for us, maybe helping with school research or game strategies without you lifting a finger.
Source: https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/892178/meta-moltbook-acquisition-ai-agents

**[Better Deepfake Checks on YouTube]**
YouTube is expanding its AI tool to detect and remove deepfakes of politicians, journalists, and officials, building on what's already there for creators. It's interesting as it shows big platforms stepping up to fight fake videos that could trick people during elections or news events, keeping online spaces safer for everyone scrolling through content.
Source: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/10/youtube-expands-ai-deepfake-detection-to-politicians-government-officials-and-journalists/

**[Meta's Deepfake Moderation Needs Work]**
The Meta Oversight Board says the company's ways of spotting and labeling deepfakes aren't strong enough, especially during conflicts like wars, and they're pushing for better systems. This is a huh-moment because it highlights how AI safety is evolving to handle real-world chaos, ensuring social media doesn't spread harmful fakes that could affect global events.
Source: https://www.theverge.com/tech/891933/meta-oversight-board-ai-labels-deepfake-c2pa-facebook-instagram
summaries.episode_title: Ep 3: Imagine chatting with an AI sidekick right inside your Google Docs to brainstorm essay ideas or build spreadsheets from scratch—it's here!

summaries.content:
# Models & Agents for Beginners
**Date:** March 09, 2026

**HOOK:** Millions are turning to AI chatbots for money advice – but experts say they're not ready to replace human pros yet.

**What's Cool Today:** Imagine asking your phone for retirement tips like you'd ask for homework help – millions are already doing it with tools like ChatGPT, but a new report warns about their limits and why you should double-check everything. We'll dive into that as our big story, explore a fresh way AI is learning to think more like a detective with probabilities, and check out hints of OpenAI's next big upgrade. Plus, a study on how juggling too many AI helpers can fry your brain, with tips to avoid it.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### The Big Story
Millions of people are now using AI chatbots like ChatGPT to get advice on things like saving for retirement or planning budgets, according to a report from the Financial Times. These tools are becoming popular because they're free, always available, and can give quick answers to tricky money questions. Think of a chatbot as a super-smart calculator mixed with a friendly advisor – it analyzes your questions about finances and pulls from tons of data to suggest plans, like how much to save each month based on your age and goals. But it's not magic; these AIs are trained on huge amounts of text from books, websites, and articles, so they can explain concepts or run simple calculations, but they don't have real-time access to your bank account or the latest market changes unless you tell them. This is a big deal because it makes financial planning feel less intimidating, especially for teens starting to think about part-time jobs or saving for college – imagine using it to figure out if that video game purchase fits your allowance budget. For students or career changers, it could spark interest in finance careers by showing how AI is changing jobs like advising or accounting. And for parents, it's a way to explore family budgeting without paying for a pro right away. But here's the personal angle: while it's exciting to get instant tips, experts warn these AIs can make mistakes, like giving outdated advice or overlooking personal details, so it's like getting suggestions from a knowledgeable friend who sometimes gets facts wrong. This might affect you by making money talks more accessible, but it also raises questions about trusting tech over humans for big decisions. What you can do right now: Head over to chat.openai.com (or the ChatGPT app on your phone) and try asking something simple like "How can a 16-year-old start saving for a car?" – then compare it to advice from a trusted adult or website like Khan Academy to see the differences. Remember to keep it general and never share real account numbers. It's a fun way to test AI's helpfulness without any risk.
Source: https://the-decoder.com/millions-already-use-ai-chatbots-for-financial-advice-but-experts-warn-of-clear-limits/

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Explain Like I'm 14
Let's unpack probabilistic reasoning, which is basically how AI can get better at updating its "beliefs" based on new clues, like a detective piecing together a mystery. Imagine you're playing a game of Clue, where you start with some guesses about who did it, but as you find more cards, you adjust your ideas – that's probabilistic reasoning, weighing odds and changing your mind with evidence. Step one: You begin with a starting guess, like "It was probably Colonel Mustard because he's sneaky," based on what you know so far. Step two: New info comes in, say a card shows Mustard was elsewhere, so you lower the odds for him and raise them for someone else, calculating how much to shift based on how strong the clue is. Step three: You keep updating with every new piece, getting closer to the truth without ignoring what you learned before. Step four: If the clues conflict, you balance them like averaging opinions from friends to decide on a group project idea. And that's basically what this new "Bayesian" teaching method from Google AI is doing when it trains large language models (LLMs, which are AIs that process and generate text like ChatGPT) – it helps them handle uncertainty better, so they don't just spit out one answer but can reason through probabilities, making them smarter for tasks like predicting weather or solving puzzles.
Source: https://www.marktechpost.com/2026/03/09/the-bayesian-upgrade-why-google-ais-new-teaching-method-is-the-key-to-llm-reasoning/

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Cool Stuff & Try This
**[OpenAI's Next Big Thing? Hints of a Super-Smart Omni Model: The Decoder]**
OpenAI might be working on a new "omni" model, which is an upgrade to their AI that handles multiple types of input like text, images, and sound all at once – think of it as evolving from a text-only chatbot to a full multimedia helper. It's cool because it could make AI even better at real-world tasks, like describing a photo you upload or generating audio from your ideas, opening up creative fun for things like making custom stories with voices or editing videos. Teens into gaming or social media should try this out, as it hints at future tools for designing game levels or remixing TikTok sounds. While the new model isn't out yet, you can get a taste of similar tech right now with their current version. Go to chat.openai.com, upload a simple drawing or photo from your phone (like a sketch of your dream pet), and ask it to "turn this into a short story with sound effects" – it's free and shows how multimodal AI (handling different media types) blends creativity in ways that feel like magic for hobbies or school projects.
Source: https://the-decoder.com/openai-employees-hint-at-a-new-omni-model/

**[Watch Out for "AI Brain Fry" – Don't Overload on Tech Helpers: The Decoder]**
A new study found that when workers juggle too many AI tools at once, like overseeing multiple chatbots or agents (which are AI programs that can perform tasks step-by-step), it leads to "brain fry" – basically mental exhaustion that causes more mistakes and even makes people want to quit. It's exciting (in a cautionary way) because it shows AI is powerful but needs to be used smartly, like not cramming too many apps into your study session. This matters for students or career changers, as it highlights how future jobs might involve managing AI without burning out, similar to balancing homework and video games. Anyone curious about productivity should give this a whirl to see the effects firsthand. Open two free AI tools on your laptop, like ChatGPT at chat.openai.com and Google Bard at bard.google.com, then try switching between them for a quick task – ask each to help plan a fun weekend activity, notice if it feels overwhelming after a few switches, and take a break to avoid that fry feeling.
Source: https://the-decoder.com/study-warns-of-ai-brain-fry-as-workers-hit-cognitive-limits-overseeing-ai-agents/

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Quick Bits
**[UK's New AI Fund Boosts Homegrown Tech]**
The UK is launching a sovereign AI fund with £500 million to build its own computing power for AI, meaning less reliance on other countries' tech – it's cool because it could lead to more accessible AI tools made right at home, like faster apps for education or games without global delays.
Source: https://www.artificialintelligence-news.com/news/uk-sovereign-ai-fund-build-domestic-computing-infrastructure/

**[Bank's AI Center Levels Up Operations]**
City Union Bank in India just teamed up to create an AI center for testing tools on real banking tasks, like automating customer help – this is neat as it shows how AI is making everyday services like loans or accounts smoother, potentially inspiring careers in tech-finance combos.
Source: https://www.artificialintelligence-news.com/news/city-union-bank-launches-ai-centre-to-support-banking-operations/
summaries.episode_title: Ep 2: Millions are turning to AI chatbots for money advice – but experts say they're not ready to replace human pros yet.

summaries.content:
# Models & Agents for Beginners
**Date:** March 09, 2026

**HOOK:** Imagine AI getting smarter by binge-watching videos like you do on TikTok—Meta says that's the future!

**What's Cool Today:** Today, we're diving into how AI might soon learn from endless videos instead of running out of text, opening up wild new possibilities for smarter helpers in games and school. We'll also compare two top AI chatbots you can test yourself for homework or creative fun, and uncover why AI tests are missing most real jobs. Stick around for a deep dive on how AI learns from unlabeled data, plus easy ways to try cutting-edge tools right now.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### The Big Story
Andrej Karpathy, a famous AI expert who helped build things like Tesla's self-driving tech, just released a simple tool called Autoresearch that lets AI systems run their own machine learning experiments without much human help. It's all in one short Python file, designed to work on a single powerful graphics card like those in gaming computers. Think of Autoresearch like a mini lab where an AI agent (a smart program that can take actions on its own, like a virtual assistant deciding steps to solve a problem) can test ideas in machine learning, which is the process of teaching computers to learn from data. It's based on a stripped-down version of another tool for training language models, but super simplified so it's easier to use and tweak. This is a big deal because it makes advanced AI research more accessible, potentially letting students or hobbyists experiment with how AIs learn without needing huge supercomputers. For example, imagine using it to train an AI to analyze your favorite video games or predict trends in social media posts. It matters to you because as AI gets into everyday stuff like school projects or creative hobbies, tools like this could inspire the next generation of inventors—maybe even a teen building their first AI model for a science fair. Personally, if you're curious about careers in tech, this shows how AI is becoming something you can tinker with at home, not just for big companies. Right now, you can check out the project on GitHub, but since it's code-based, start by reading the simple readme file to understand the basics—search for "Autoresearch Karpathy GitHub" in your browser. If you're not ready for code, watch Karpathy's free YouTube videos on AI basics to get inspired. For a hands-on taste, try chatting with a free AI like ChatGPT about what machine learning experiments you'd run if you had an AI lab.
Source: https://www.marktechpost.com/2026/03/08/andrej-karpathy-open-sources-autoresearch-a-630-line-python-tool-letting-ai-agents-run-autonomous-ml-experiments-on-single-gpus/

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Explain Like I'm 14
Let's explain how AI models learn from unlabeled video data, a hot idea from today's news that's changing how we build smarter AIs. Imagine you're trying to learn a new sport like soccer just by watching hours of pro games on YouTube without any coach explaining the rules—you pick up patterns like how players pass the ball or score goals by noticing what happens over and over. Step one: The AI starts with raw videos, broken into tiny clips, kind of like flipping through a flipbook where each page is a frame. Step two: It looks for connections within those frames, like spotting that a ball moving left often leads to a player running right, building a map of "what usually happens next" without needing labels like "this is a goal." Step three: To make it multimodal (meaning it handles different types of data like text and images together), the AI mixes in text descriptions, linking video patterns to words so it can later answer questions like "describe this soccer play." Step four: Over tons of examples, it refines this map, getting better at predicting and understanding without being told exactly what everything means. And that's basically what new AI training methods are doing when they use unlabeled videos—they're letting models learn the world's patterns from visual data alone, which could make AIs way better at real-life tasks like helping with homework videos or creating game animations.
Source: General concept

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Cool Stuff & Try This
**[Gemini vs. Claude: Which AI Wins at Real-Life Tasks?]: Latest from Tom's Guide**  
Gemini 3 Flash and Claude Sonnet 4.6 are two powerful AI chatbots—think of them as super-smart virtual friends that can reason, plan, write stories, or spark creative ideas based on your prompts (short instructions you give them). The article tested them on everyday challenges like planning a trip or writing a poem, and surprisingly, Claude edged out in creativity while Gemini was faster for quick answers. This is cool because it shows how AIs are getting better at stuff you actually do, like brainstorming school essays or designing game characters, without needing fancy tech knowledge. Teens and students should try this to see AI in action for homework help or fun projects—it's like having a tireless study buddy. Access them for free: Go to gemini.google.com for Gemini (sign in with a Google account) or anthropic.com/claude for Claude (create a free account). Right now, try this creative challenge—ask Gemini to "plan a fantasy adventure story based on my favorite video game," then ask Claude the same and compare which one feels more exciting; it'll take just 5 minutes and might inspire your next TikTok video or art project.  
Source: https://www.tomsguide.com/ai/i-ran-7-real-world-prompts-on-gemini-3-and-claude-sonnet-4-6-the-results-surprised-me

**[AI Benchmarks Miss Most Jobs—Time for a Change?]: The Decoder**  
A new study reveals that tests for AI agents (programs that act independently to complete tasks, like an AI booking your travel) focus almost entirely on coding problems, ignoring 92% of real US jobs like teaching, nursing, or retail. This is exciting because it highlights how AI could expand to help with everyday careers, not just tech ones, potentially making tools for things like organizing school events or managing a band's social media. Curious beginners and career explorers should check this out to think about AI's future impact on non-tech fields. While there's no direct tool to try, read the full story for inspiration. For a quick experiment, head to chat.openai.com (free with signup) and ask ChatGPT to "simulate an AI agent helping a teacher plan a lesson"—see how it handles non-coding tasks and imagine improving those benchmarks yourself someday.  
Source: https://the-decoder.com/ai-agent-benchmarks-obsess-over-coding-while-ignoring-92-of-the-us-labor-market-study-finds/

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
### Quick Bits
**[Text Data Running Low? Videos to the Rescue!]**  
Researchers from Meta and NYU trained an AI from scratch using images and text, discovering that old assumptions about training don't always hold, and unlabeled videos could be the huge new source of data as text runs dry. This is super interesting because it means AIs might soon learn from the endless stream of online videos, making them smarter at understanding real-world stuff like your favorite YouTube clips or social media trends—imagine an AI that gets memes without explanations!  
Source: https://the-decoder.com/llm-text-data-is-drying-up-but-meta-points-to-unlabeled-video-as-the-next-massive-training-frontier/
summaries.episode_title: Ep 1: Imagine AI getting smarter by binge-watching videos like you do on TikTok—Meta says that's the future!
